coredata.dc:title: Electro-chemo-mechanical behavior of a layered cathode material upon cycling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Acta Materialia Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Acta Materialia Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Löser, Robert
Joshi, Yug
Talei, Roham
Ebbinghaus, Petra
Schmitz, Guido
coredata.dc:description: The mechanical characteristics of lithium-ion cathode materials plays a critical role in determining battery performance such as durability, cycle life, and safety, especially when the battery is under external pressure which is typical for all-solid-state batteries. This study focuses on LiCoO2 (LCO), a widely used hexagonal layer-structured cathode material for lithium-ion batteries, and investigates its mechanical properties during de-/lithiation using sputter-deposited thin films and nanoindentation. The values of the experimental Young’s modulus in pure (101) and (003) lattice orientations are quantified to 337.1 ± 8.7 GPa and 267.9 ± 7.2 GPa, respectively, in the fully lithiated state. Furthermore, a substantial texture-dependent decrease in Young’s modulus upon lithium deintercalation is demonstrated, probably due to modification of the bonding interactions between the cobalt oxide layers. The decrease of Li content also elevates the relative contribution of plastic deformation, indicating that dislocation glide becomes easier in deintercalated states. By extensive cycling, the Young’s modulus in higher lithiated charge-states decreases considerably which is most-likely due to irreversibility of phase transitions. In contrast, the material shows a significant increase of plastic hardness with cycling, understood as work hardening. The work provides valuable insight on the dynamic changes of the mechanical properties during electrochemical cycling of LiCoO2, which paves the way for all other layered cathode materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium cobalt oxide
Mechanics
Electrochemical fatigue
Young modulus
Nano-indentation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanical properties in the fully lithiated state
Elastic anisotropy in partly delithiated states

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of Acta Materialia Inc.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) in the frame of a large investment fund for the new transmission microscope (INST 41/1096\u20131 FUGG, Project number 419370052) and a project fund dedicated to using battery materials as active actuators (SCHM 1182/ 29\u20131, Photoelektromat, Project number 504493661).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Theoretically [4–9] and experimentally [10–12] determined Young's modulus values of fully lithiated LCO as reported in the literature. Dash-dotted lines represent the range of determined Young’s moduli within a single study [4]. used lattice model calculation, [5,7,8] used DFT calculations, [6,9] used first principle calculations to determine the theoretical values. Experimental studies [11] investigated thin films of LCO, [10] individual particles and [12] hot-pressed powder to evaluate the Young’s modulus experimentally. For the DFT studies [13,14], the Voigt-Reuss-homogenization (Equation S1-S7) has been calculated out of the elastic constants. The red stars indicate the range of results in this study, dependent on the texture.
a) Schematic representation of the sputtered layers. b) Three-electrode setup used for electrochemical analysis.
A representative force displacement graph obtained from a nanoindentation measurement. Herein, h and ℎ0 are used to estimate the elastic depth recovery ratio.

a) X-ray diffractogram of a sputter deposited, annealed LCO sample (film thickness 700 nm, Bragg-Brentano theta-2-theta geometry, Cu Kα irradiation). b) Cross section TEM bright field image of the corresponding microstructure (film thickness 700 nm).

a) Cyclic voltammetry of a 700 nm thick LCO film at cycles 1, 2 and 20 conducted at 0.3 mV/s. b) Calculated volumetric capacity during 100 cycles of the 700 nm thick LCO layer, employing the CV shown in a. c) Galvanostatic de-/charging curves of cycle 1, 10 and 100. d) Galvanostatic delithiation in the 10th cycle with selected lithium concentrations and voltages correlated.

Young’s modulus of a 700 nm thick, sputter-deposited LCO samples. After cycling 1, 10 and 100 times the next delithiation half cycle was interrupted at different lithium concentrations shown at the x-axis. The behavior of the 1 Cycle sample is linearly fitted by the dotted magenta line. Blue and black dotted lines visualize the predicted Young’s modulus after 10 and 100 cycles using Eq. (5). The corresponding error is shown via the colored bands.
Raman spectra of an as-prepared and a 100 times cycled LCO sample.

a) Experimentally determined Young’s modulus in fully lithiated state for different layer thicknesses, comparing the influence of substrate and texture. b) (003) volume fraction of LCO samples with different layer thicknesses, including interpolating linear fit (dashed). The hollow blue dots represent the corresponding volume fraction after post-annealing.

a) Measured and corrected Young’s modulus of 300 nm (preferred (003) orientation) and 900 nm (preferred (101) orientation) thick LCO layers, upon delithiation. Results of DFT calculation [14] of the Young’s modulus perpendicular to the (001) and (101) planes are shown for comparison, magenta and blue line, respectively; b) Schematic view of the (101) plane direction as blue plane and (001) plane direction as magenta plane in the LiCoO2 crystal structure, where blue balls are Co, green balls are Li and red balls are oxygen [50].

a) Young’s modulus and hardness of a 500 nm thick LCO sample in comparison using an indentation depth of 50 nm; b) Hardness of a 500 nm and 900 nm thick LCO sample with 50 nm and 100 nm indentation depth.
Hardness of a 700 nm thick, sputter deposited LCO samples, cycled 1, 10 and 100 times, with different lithium concentrations.
Elastic recovery ratio and estimated plastic resistance of a 700 nm thick, sputter deposited LCO layer, upon delithiation.
Young’s moduli of fully lithiated LCO in (003) and (101) orientation, determined by solving a linear equation system using different pairs of layer thicknesses.
Comparison of experimentally and theoretically determined Young's moduli for the (003) and (101) direction for the fully lithiated sample.

head.ce:title: Electro-chemo-mechanical behavior of a layered cathode material upon cycling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Physics, Institute for Materials Science, University of Stuttgart, Heisenbergstraße 3, 70569 Stuttgart, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Physics, Institute for Materials Science, University of Stuttgart

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Materials Physics, Institute for Materials Science, Universität Stuttgart, Heisenbergstraße 3, 70569 Stuttgart, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Max Planck Institute for Sustainable Materials, Max-Planck-Straße 1, 40237 Dusseldorf, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Max Planck Institute for Sustainable Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMax-Planck-Institut für Nachhaltige Materialien GmbH (formerly Max-Planck-Institut für Eisenforschung GmbH), Max-Planck-Straße 1, 40237 Düsseldorf, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanical characteristics of lithium-ion cathode materials plays a critical role in determining battery performance such as durability, cycle life, and safety, especially when the battery is under external pressure which is typical for all-solid-state batteries. This study focuses on LiCoO2 (LCO), a widely used hexagonal layer-structured cathode material for lithium-ion batteries, and investigates its mechanical properties during de-/lithiation using sputter-deposited thin films and nanoindentation. The values of the experimental Young’s modulus in pure (101) and (003) lattice orientations are quantified to 337.1 ± 8.7 GPa and 267.9 ± 7.2 GPa, respectively, in the fully lithiated state. Furthermore, a substantial texture-dependent decrease in Young’s modulus upon lithium deintercalation is demonstrated, probably due to modification of the bonding interactions between the cobalt oxide layers. The decrease of Li content also elevates the relative contribution of plastic deformation, indicating that dislocation glide becomes easier in deintercalated states. By extensive cycling, the Young’s modulus in higher lithiated charge-states decreases considerably which is most-likely due to irreversibility of phase transitions. In contrast, the material shows a significant increase of plastic hardness with cycling, understood as work hardening. The work provides valuable insight on the dynamic changes of the mechanical properties during electrochemical cycling of LiCoO2, which paves the way for all other layered cathode materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Upon cycling of lithium-ion cathode materials, such as the generic LiCoO2 (LCO), general volume expansion and the excess volume of phase transitions can build up considerable stress and lead therewith to fracture and failure of the cathode electrode[1,2]. This factor becomes especially important in the context of all-solid-state batteries where the rigid solid structure hinders relaxation, making stresses even more relevant [1,3]. Aiming to avoid undesired stress-induced fracture, it is crucial to investigate the corresponding mechanical properties of related materials comprehensively. However, despite this relevance, the literature lacks experimental data about the mechanical properties of LCO upon delithiation. Although theoretical studies have employed Density Functional Theory (DFT) and other theoretical concepts to ascertain mechanical properties during delithiation[4–9], the reported experimental data have solely focused on the fully lithiated state [10–12]. Even restricted to the fully lithiated material, the experimentally determined values of Young’s modulus published after 2010 widely scatter between 151 GPa [10] and 325GPa [11] as can be clearly visualized in Fig. 1 .

This study addresses this gap of information and reveals fundamental insights into the mechanical behavior across various charging states of LCO. This is done by utilizing nanoindentation on thin, sputter-deposited LCO layers in which the lithium concentration is regulated by an external voltage/potential applied to an electrochemical cell. A significant benefit of using thin-film electrodes is that they are devoid of any contribution from additives, thereby providing characteristic information of the material. In contrast to powder particles, uniform thin layers offer a planar surface for transparent hardness testing, and control of their texture enables the investigation of mechanical anisotropy.

The process of lithium de/-intercalation in LCO induces multiple phase transitions. Li1≥
x
≥ 0.975CoO2 (O3-I phase) occurs in a hexagonal structure (R3̅m space group) with Li and Co occupying the octahedral sides between the oxygen layers. Another single-phase regions appears for 50 % <x Li <75 % (metallic conductive O3-II phase), while between 97.5 % and 75 %, a heterogeneous mixture of these two phases is found [15]. To prevent irreversible phase changes, LCO should not be delithiated below x Li=50 %.

In this study, the Young’s modulus, hardness and the elastic recovery ratio upon delithiation have been measured and are, to the best of our knowledge, reported here for the first time. A remarkable change in the mechanical behavior upon intercalation or deintercalation has been observed after excessive cycling. Furthermore, the anisotropy of the elastic stiffness of LCO has been quantified.

Thin films were deposited in a custom-made ultra-high vacuum deposition chamber equipped with a 13.56 MHz radio frequency ion beam source (Rau & Roth, 4 cm in diameter). The deposition chamber maintained a base pressure of < 1 × 10⁻⁷ mbar. For the multilayered thin-film deposition, a platinum disk and a cold-pressed, sintered LiCoO2 powder (Alfa Aesar, 97 % pure, Germany) were used as targets. Oxidized silicon wafer (Siltronic AG, <100> orientation, Germany) were used as substrates. Firstly, about 80 nm thick platinum layer was deposited at a substrate temperature of 400 °C using pure argon plasma. For the deposition of LiCoO2, oxygen was added as a reactant gas, with an Ar:O ratio of 7:3 and the temperature was further increased to 450 °C. This resulted in the deposition rate of 1.9 nm/min. The thickness of the LCO layer was varied in the range of 300 to 900 nm. The schematic of the film geometry is shown in Fig. 2 a. Subsequently, the deposited bilayers were annealed at 750 °C for 1 h in ambient atmosphere, to make them electrochemically active. A prior report, using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and the same target and sputter chamber, has confirmed that the electron energy loss spectra of the films and the target match very well, indicating the same chemical composition and oxidation state [16]. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the LCO films produced remain stable until 850 °C [17]. Thus, the applied annealing temperature is not expected to alter the stoichiometry of the films.

The structure of the thin films was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu-Kα radiation generated at 40 kV and 35 mA in a Bruker D8 diffractometer. XRD spectra were obtained in θ−2θ geometry which reveals the lattice planes lying parallel to the film surface. In addition, for a quantitative determination of the texture of the thin-films, texture scans have been conducted using a parallel beam and a fixed scattering angle according to the (003) or the (101) lattice spacing. The sample normal has been incrementally tilted (2° steps) from α = 0° to 88° with respect to the incidence plane of the beam. For each tilting angle α, azimuthal averaging was performed by full circle rotation (ϕ = 0° … 360°) around the film normal. Exemplary texture plots showing intensity versus polar and azimuthal angle, are presented in the supplementary information, see Fig. S2–5.

Furthermore, microstructural characterization was conducted through TEM using a Thermo Fischer Scientific Spectra 300 TEM operating at 200 kV. Electron-transparent TEM specimens were prepared using the established focused-ion beam (FIB) lift-out technique with a FEI SCIOS dual beam instrument.

In addition, Raman spectra were recorded using a WiTec Alpha 300 M confocal Raman microscope equipped with an 1800 grooves/mm diffraction grating and a 50x magnification objective. The excitation source was a laser operating at a wavelength of 633 nm. The laser power at the sample plane was approximately 16 mW and the exposure time was set to 10 × 2 s each.

For electrochemical cycling and for preparing a particular state of charge (SOC) of LiCoO2, a three-electrode cell with a liquid electrolyte has been used. Fig. 2b shows the schematic of the electrochemical setup used in this study. As reference and counter electrodes, lithium metal foils were employed. All the voltages are with respect to Li, unless otherwise mentioned. The electrolyte consisted of 1 M lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, Sigma Aldrich, 99.99 %) dissolved in 1:1 (mass ratio) of ethylene carbonate (EC, Alpha Aesar, 99 %) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC, Sigma Aldrich, ≥ 99 %).

Electrochemical characterization was carried out inside an argon-filled glovebox (MBraun model unilab, Germany) with humidity and oxygen impurity levels below 1 ppm. All electrochemical measurements were conducted at room temperature using a potentiostat (Bio-Logic VSP-300) within a potential window between 3.4 to 4.2 V vs Li/Li+ (the upper boundary is limited to 4.2 V to avoid irreversible phase transformations [18,19]). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was executed at a scan rate of 0.3 mV/s.

The mechanical properties were investigated by utilizing a Nanoindenter XP (Agilent, California, USA) fitted with a cube corner diamond indenter tip (Micro Star Technology, USA). Each datapoint represents an average of at least 125 indents to guarantee a statistically significant result. The conducted experiments were exclusively depth-controlled indentation measurements with a maximum indentation depth of 50 nm, using indenters with an apex radius of 20 nm. The elastic modulus and Poisson’s ratio of the diamond indenter are 1140 GPa and 0.07, respectively [20], while the Poisson ratio for LCO was assumed to be 0.24 [12]. The resulting load-displacement curves of each indentation (Fig. 3 shows a representative load-displacement curve) were analyzed for the hardness and Young’s modulus using the established Oliver-Pharr method [21]:
(1)
H =
P max
A
Where H is the indentation hardness, P max the peak indentation load and A the contact area, which is determined from the known tip geometry and the indentation depth.

For the determination of the Young’s modulus, the experimentally measured stiffness S of the upper part of the unloading curve is used to calculate the reduced modulus
E r
(see Fig. 3).
(2.1)
S =
d P
d h
=
2
π
·
E r
·
A
(2.2)
1
E r
=
1 −
v
s
2
E s
+
1 −
v
i
2
E i
Where E s and vs are the Young’s modulus and the Poisson’s ratio of the sample, respectively. E i and v i are Young’s modulus and Poisson’s ratio of the indenter. Note, that the measured raw (reduced) modulus is dependent on the elasticity of both the sample and the indenter.

The resistance to plastic deformation was characterized by the appropriate ratio between hardness (H) and Young’s modulus (E) [22,23]:
(3)
Plastic
Resistance
∼
H
3
E
2

Additionally, the elastic recovery ratio (ERR) has been determined, which represents the proportion of elastic deformation to the total deformation [24]:
(4)
ERR
=
h −
h 0
h
Here, h represents the maximum indentation depth, while h 0 corresponds to the indentation depth after unloading the tip. Consequently, h 0 is the deformation after elastic recovery and, as such, represents the plastic deformation (Fig. 3). For quantification of the elastic recovery ratio, the average ERR values obtained from 10 selected indents has been used. These selected indents showed Young’s modulus values within ± 2 % of the average Young's modulus calculated from the full set of 125 indents.

An XRD of a 700 nm thick representative sample layer is shown in Fig. 4 a. Besides the strong peaks at 39.9° for the platinum (111) plane and at 69.1° for the silicon (100) plane, significant peaks can be seen for (003) and (101) planes of LCO at 18.9° and 38.4°, respectively. Except that no discernible peak for a possible (104) orientation is seen, these observations are in good agreement with the textures of LCO typically achieved through sputter deposition [25]. Furthermore, the Kβ peak of (003) LCO, (111) Pt and (400) Si can be observed at 17.1°, 35.8° and 61.7°, respectively. The minor peaks detected between 50°and 60° cannot assign to a specific peak for the used materials. Consequently, they are not discussed further, as no LCO related peaks are anticipated within this region. The Bragg-Brentano XRD indicates that the overwhelming part of the volume is oriented either with (003) or (101) planes parallel to the film surface. Since the relative contributions of these two orientations vary with the film thickness, they were quantitatively determined in later shown texture measurements.

In addition to XRD, the microstructure of the produced LCO layers has been characterized using TEM. A bright field image is depicted in Fig. 4b. It reveals a dense layer of LCO with a lateral grain diameter of about 80 nm. Columnar growth of the LCO can be observed with grains partly spreading almost over the full thickness of the layer, which is typical for a sputtered film and may depend in detail on the orientation of the substrate as well as on surface diffusion and ion-energy during the deposition processes [26].

Fig. 5 a illustrates cyclic voltammograms for the 1st, 2nd and 20th cycle of a representative thin LCO film after thermal annealing. Observed current peaks are consistent with previously reported data for pure LCO [27]. The deintercalation peak (or positive current side) exhibits minor shift between the 2nd (4.00 V) and 20th cycle (4.013 V), whereas the intercalation peak demonstrates even smaller change between the 1st/2nd (3.84 V) and the 20th (3.83 V) cycles. These slight shifts in peak position can be ascribed to several factors, including the formation of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) [28], structural and interphase alterations of LCO [29] and irreversible phase transitions, which may occur despite restricting the upper voltage limit to 4.2 V [30]. Especially Li loss owing to SEI formation at the counter electrode or CEI formation at the sample surface is probably responsible for the significant decrease in peak height between the 1st and 2nd cycle (Fig. 5b). Between the 2nd and 20th cycle capacity fading slows down, as confirmed by the plot of capacity versus cycle number in Fig. 5b A quantitative examination reveals a gradual decline from the 2nd to the 100th cycle (capacity loss of about 0.5 % per cycle. Furthermore, the measured capacity is notably lower than the theoretical value of 69 µAh/cm²∙µm [31]. This discrepancy can be attributed to increased electrode/electrolyte interactions caused by the high surface area of the thin film which is also reported literature [32]. Additionally, the significant presence of (003)-oriented lattice planes, aligned parallel to the surface, restricts lithium-ion intercalation and deintercalation. As investigated by [33], this capacity fading can also be explained by partially oxidizing of the thin film LCO during cycling. The same trend is also seen by the galvanostatic treatment at constant ±8.32 µA/cm2, depicted in Fig. 5c, where a noticeable reduction in cycle duration with increasing cycle number is evident. The observable plateau at around 3.9–4.0 V during charging and discharging can be explained by the occurring phase transition, changing the cobalt oxidation state from Co3+ to Co4+[34]. Furthermore, it has to be noted that the kinetics and potentials of lithium insertion in LCO may be influenced by the texture of the sample, given the anticipated anisotropic diffusion rates of lithium into the layered atomic arrangement [35].

In addition to the characterization of electrochemical performance, the electrochemical methodology has also been utilized to select various voltages and prepare films with the corresponding lithium concentrations. For this, it is assumed that the lithium content in the as-sputtered state corresponds to 100 % and changes linearly with time upon galvanostatic delithiation until reaching a lithium content of 50 % at 4.2 V vs. Li+/Li [36–38]. As depicted in Fig. 5d for the case of the 10th delithiation half cycle of a galvanostatic treatment, certain electrochemical potentials are correlated to predefined lithium concentrations. After reaching the selected voltages in the galvanostatic process, the potential was held constant for 14 h to equilibrate the Li distribution. In this way, different states of charge were prepared for the mechanical measurements, while maintaining consistent use of the same reference and counter electrode as well as electrolyte, which are always kept in the Ar protected glovebox.

Beginning from the as-deposited state (fully lithiated), nanoindentation was performed at different delithiation states. Fig. 6 shows the change in Young’s modulus upon delithiation after a pre-treatment of 1, 10 or 100 charging cycles (magenta, yellow and blue data points, respectively). Overall, a significant decrease in Young’s modulus upon delithiation can be seen in all three cycling states. This principal behavior can be basically attributed to a modulation in the interlayer bonding [7]. In the fully lithiated state, interlayer interactions are dominated by strong coulombic forces between the negatively charged cobalt-oxide layers and the positively charged lithium layers. By delithiation, this strong interaction changes to weaker van der Waals interactions between cobalt-oxide layers, and thus, leading to a reduction in Young’s modulus [7].

With regard to battery life, a pronounced decrease in the Young's modulus with increasing cycle number is seen (compare magenta, yellow and blue data points), especially for Li contents x Li > 75 %, while only negligible change with cycling appears for low Li contents (x Li < 75 %).

In quantitative terms, the Young’s modulus in the fully lithiated state reduces from the 1st to the 10th cycle and from the 10th to the 100th cycle by 3.3 % and 8 %, respectively. Thus, a natural explanation for the observed reduction of stiffness with cycling would be a general loss of lithium content during battery life5. By assuming that both Young's modulus and capacity are solely dependent on the available lithium content, the probable impact of lithium loss on the mechanical properties could be assessed. To this end, the results after one cycle, at which the available Li content is still close to the as-prepared state has been linearly fitted to estimate the dependence of stiffness on the lithium content (see magenta dotted line Fig. 6):
(5)
E Y
= n +
x Li
· m

In which E Y represents the resulting Young’s modulus, n is the hypothetical Young’s modulus in complete absence of Li (extrapolated to 196.9 GPa from the 1 Cycle data),
x Li
is the lithium fraction and m quantifies the sensitivity of Young’s modulus on the Li content (interpolated to 86.6 GPa from the 1 Cycle data).

Consequently, the relative capacity loss of 7.6 % between the 1st and the 10th cycle (see Fig. 5b) would estimate the maximum available Li content after 10 cycles to 96.2 % (capacity loss is stated with regard to the minimum concentration of x Li=50 %). Employing Eq. (5), a Young’s modulus of 281.1 GPa can be predicted (see black dotted line and point Fig. 6), not that far from the experimental value of 275.9 GPa measured after 10 cycles. The capacity loss of 54.1 % (Fig. 1b) indicates a maximum Li content of 73 %, which predicts the elastic modulus to be 255.3 GPa (see blue line and point Fig. 6), in perfect agreement to the 255.5 GPa actually measured. Consequently, all estimations derived in this way from the capacity decrease align well with the measured values observed in the cycled samples and within the appropriate margin of error. This strongly suggests that most of the change in Young’s modulus upon cycling is due to the decreasing intercalation of Li.

Nevertheless, the detailed behavior of the elastic modulus on cathode cycling, points out a change of mechanism for Li content of x Li ≤ 75 %. This indicates that the primary interpretation may still be too simple. In general, the cycling behavior of the cathode material could be understood by several processes, such as SEI/CEI formation, structural degradation (especially cracks) and formation of point defects, namely e.g. H + substitution of Li+. Although these factors could affect the mechanical properties, they hardly explain the observed abrupt change of behavior at a characteristic content of x Li=75 %.

A meaningful interpretation is obtained with regard to the phase transformation between the two different hexagonal structures and its reversibility, since the observed characteristic Li content just marks completion of the transformation.

Delithiation of LCO below 97.5 % induces an isomorphic transition of the hexagonal insulating Li1≥
x
≥ 0.975CoO2 (O3-I) phase into the metallic conductive Li0.75≥
x
≥ 0.5CoO2 (O3-II) phase. The phase transformation leads to a different ordering of Li atoms within the lithium planes of the hexagonal structure, which likely also has an impact on the interlayer coupling and therefore the elastic modulus. So, one may correlate the major reduction of the elastic modulus at concentrations x Li>75 % with the relative increase of the O3-II phase fraction within the two-phase range of the phase diagram. In pure O3-II (below x Li < 75 %), the elastic modulus is obviously relatively low and only weakly depending on the Li content.

In this interpretation, the mechanical behavior of the LCO layers during cycling consequently indicates that the phase transition between O3-I and O3-II is not fully reversible. While the transformation into O3-II is completed, the reverse transition to O3-I becomes more and more limited so that the large moduli of the pure O3-I phase are not reached any more. This may be due to a general loss of Li out of the structure or due to a too slow diffusion of Li ions to the Li layer between the Co-O octahedra. In addition to insufficient Li for the phase transformation, the irreversibility of the phase transformation could also be due to considerable structural degradation and/or internal stresses [39] and therewith particle cracks and particle pulverization [40]. But most important, it has to be noted, that after a few cycles, it is not possible to attain the pure O3-I phase, as the required lithium content of 97.5 % cannot be reached anymore.

To validate this irreversible phase transitions experimentally, Raman spectroscopy was performed on an as-prepared and a 100 times cycled sample (see Fig. 7 ). Here, a clear shift in the Eg and A1g Raman active modes is revealed after excessive cycling. After 100 cycles, the Eg mode shifts from 484.3 cm-1 to 475.9 cm-1, while the A1g mode shifts from 593.2 cm-1 to 584.2 cm-1. This shift corresponds to the formation of a coexisting O3-I and O3-II phase, with a lithium concentration between 70 % and 90 % [41]. Since the observed sample was electrochemically lithiated to the maximum lithium concentration, the Raman spectroscopy results confirm the occurrence of an irreversible phase transformation and a limited maximum lithium concentration after excessive cycling. Note, that the Raman peak at around 690 cm-1 corresponds to Co3O4 [41,42]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties in the fully lithiated state
ce:section.ce:para: After having achieved an overview on the impact of cycling, we focus on the Young's modulus of the fully lithiated LCO films in samples that were processed with 10 cycles. Investigating the samples after a few electrochemical charging cycles has the obvious advantage of a well normalized, and reproducible lithiation state, although we need to accept that the elastic modulus might already be reduced by 10 GPa (about 3 %, see Fig. 6) in comparison to the pristine materials. Surprisingly, a significant variation of stiffness with the film thickness is noticed, see Fig. 8 a, the blue triangles.

As diffractometry reveals a systematic variation of the (003) and (101) peak intensities with thickness, we may suspect a change of the texture being responsible for variation of stiffness. Given that only (101) and (003) orientations are observable in the Bragg-Brentano XRD (see Fig. 4a), the relative volume fractions were exclusively determined between these two orientational variants. To clarify our hypothesis by quantitative arguments, texture scans have been evaluated to determine the corresponding volumed
V 003
and
V 101
of (003) and (101) lattice planes lying parallel to the surface. To this end, we compared the intensity measured in the inner range of polar angles (0° ≤
α
≤ 10°) to the total intensity (0° <
α
≤ 90°) which normalizes for potential variation in beam intensity, exposure area and time of X-ray measurement. In addition, we need to take into account the different scattering strength of both Bragg reflections by appropriate sensitivity factors
c
h k l
that were calibrated from LCO powder patterns with random particle orientation: [34]
(6)
c hkl
c
h
'
k
'
l
'
=
I hkl
I
h
'
k
'
l
'
where
I
h k l
is the respective peak intensity measured in a powder spectrum (c 003=1, c 101=0.83).

With this, the corresponding volume fractions
ρ 001
and
ρ 101
of the (001) and (101) planes parallel to film surface, can be calculated:
(7)
ρ 001
≔
V 001
V 001
+
V 101
=
I 003
C 003
I 003
C 003
+
I 101
C 101
;
ρ 101
≔
V 101
V 001
+
V 101
=
I 101
C 101
I 003
C 003
+
I 101
C 101

By employing texture scans for various layer thicknesses, the volume fractions were determined versus the thickness as presented in Fig. 8b An almost linear dependence of the grain orientation on the thickness (dashed straight line), from dominating (003) orientation in very thin layers to more pronounced (101) orientation in thicker layers. This tendency is in accordance with literature [43]. Furthermore, control experiments with post-annealing at the temperature of deposition (450 °C) demonstrate that the significant change in texture is majorly a consequence of the longer heat treatment during longer sputter duration of the thicker films.

Besides the varying texture, one may consider possible other factors provoking a thickness dependence. In nanoindentation of thin films, the substrate may have a considerable impact on the measured mechanical properties. A guiding estimation for the influence of the substrate is provided by Bückle’s rule, which states that with a ratio of 1:10 between indentation depth and layer thickness, the impact of the substrate can be neglected in measurements of hardness [44]. In case of the elastic modulus, the impact of the substrate can be more pronounced. So, with an indentation depth of 50 nm, it potentially still affects the presented measurements of the 700 nm thick LCO layer [45]. Given that the Young’s moduli of Pt (170 GPa [46]) and of the silicon wafer (160 GPa [47]) are significantly lower than the expected Young’s modulus of LCO, this substrate effect is expected to lower the measured Young’s modulus artificially.

It is however reasonably assumed that all substrate effects are negligible for thicknesses of 900 nm or greater [45]. Therefore, to identify the possible influence of the substrate in our experiments, thinner films have been further annealed directly after deposition, still inside the sputter chamber, to reach the same heating duration as the 900 nm thick films experience during sputtering, and so to ensure a similar texture. The latter was confirmed by XRD texture scans. E.g. a 500 nm thick LCO sample was prepared by 4.4 h deposition followed by subsequent annealing at 450 °C for further 3.5 h, as the typical deposition time for 900 nm thick LCO amounts to 7.9 h. The Young’s moduli of these post-annealed samples are visualized in Fig. 8a as magenta dots.

The obtained relative volume ratios after the extended annealing are depicted in Fig. 8b (hollow blue dots). Obviously, any systematic variation has been successfully suppressed. Only fluctuations in the texture are observed. Consequently, for this data set, the remaining systematic increase of Young’s modulus with layer thicknesses (Fig. 8a, magenta data points) must be due to the elastic substrate effect and eventual further unknown influences that can, however, be summarized as effective substrate effects.

If the variation of the elastic modulus remains moderate, substrate and texture effects superpose additively to the blue triangle data points. So, knowing the substrate effect, it can be subtracted to separate the pure texture dependency of the LCO films as presented by the green data points (Fig. 8a).

Given a columnar grain structure and the main loading direction perpendicular to surface, the measured Young’s modulus represents an average of (003) and (101) oriented grains, weighted by their respective volume fractions (Fig. 8b full blue dots). Since the latter are known, the individual Young’s moduli for loads perpendicular to the (003) and to the (101) planes can be determined by solving the corresponding linear equation systems (see supporting information). Finally, since the Young’s modulus for the fully lithiated state has already slightly decreased after 10 cycles (see Fig. 6), the determined results should better be corrected by +3.3 %. The so-determined and corrected results are shown in Table 1 . This full quantification determines the Young’s modulus for (003) oriented LCO to 267.9 ± 7.2 GPa, while (101) oriented LCO has a Young’s modulus of 337.1 ± 8.7 GPa. These experimental quantitative values represent one of the main experimental results of this study. Note that the stated errors represent statistical uncertainty estimated from scatter between 6 repetitions of XRD texture scans at the example of the 700 nm thick LCO layer and repetition of 300 nano-indentations. The same error margins have been applied to all other data points, visualized by the color bands.

To further confirm our quantitative interpretation, also the model of Gao [48] has been used to calculate and subtract the substrate effect in comparison (equations S10 to S14). The so-corrected Young’s modulus values for different layer thicknesses can be compared with the experimentally determined ones (Fig. 8a black dots). The comparison demonstrated an excellent agreement, with a maximum deviation of only 3.4 % for the example of the 700 nm thick sample.

This brings us in position to check the validity of available DFT studies. The theoretical Young’s moduli for the studied lattice orientations can be calculated from published DFT data, if these present the full set of 4 independent elastic constants that are required to calculate the orientation-dependent Young modulus of hexagonal structures [49] (see Supplementary material). A comparison of various theoretical studies with our experimental data is shown in Table 2 .

The Young’s moduli calculated from the DFT elastic constants of Qi et al. [5] were the closest to the experimentally determined ones, with 224.4 GPa for (003) (−16.2 %) slightly too low and with 365.7 GPa for (101) (+ 7.8 %), just slightly too high. Consequently, with ΔE = 141.3 GPa, the absolute difference between both orientations is overestimated. By contrast, Sailuam et al. [9] reported lower absolute values of Young’s modulus, while the predicted difference between the (003) and (101) orientations matches with 84.3 GPa quite reasonably to our experimental observations (69.2 ± 11.3 GPa). In general, the experimental measurements of the Young's modulus are in reasonable agreement with the values predicted by theoretical models. However, the difference between the (003) and (101) orientations is less pronounced and aligns with the lower end of theoretical predictions.

Moreover, these findings may be compared with those reported in other experimental studies (see Fig. 1). In this context, the Young’s modulus values obtained in the present study generally lie toward the upper bound of the observed range. Since the stiffness depends strongly on the lithiation grade, one may first assume the lithiation grades of the experimentally investigated materials were not fully identical. In this sense, a higher Young’s modulus would speak for better lithiation. Second, it is striking that experimental data from another thin film study [11] also tend to rather high elastic modulus. Thus, potentially thin film materials reveal a higher density than sintered powder materials produced by solid state chemistry. Higher density and less porosity are likely to produce higher elastic modulus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elastic anisotropy in partly delithiated states
ce:section.ce:para: The Young’s modulus of the stepwise delithiated LCO samples with different thickness is presented in Fig. 9 a. For comparison, LCO layers with thicknesses of 300 nm and 900 nm are shown, exhibiting the maximum volume fractions of the two preferred grain orientations of 93 % (003) and 61 % (101), respectively. Note, that the Young’s modulus of the 300 nm thick layer has been corrected for the substrate effect by the model of Gao [48]. Both the absolute Young’s modulus values and the changes upon delithiation differ considerably between the differently oriented samples. The (101) direction generally exhibits greater stiffness with only a minor decrease upon delithiation, whereas the (003) direction shows a more pronounced change (see Fig. 9a). This can be naturally explained by the fact that with indentation perpendicular to the layered structure (003) orientation, lattice planes of the largest interatomic distances are compressed. Similarly, the higher stiffness variation with lithium content in this orientation can be explained, as lithium removal results in considerably larger interatomic distances between the (003) planes (see Fig. 9b).

To compare the experimentally obtained anisotropic mechanical behavior in partly delithitated LCO with the literature, the theoretical DFT calculations of Yamakawa et al. for different lithium contents have been employed [7,14]. Notably, the Young’s modulus for the (101) and (003) directions are dependent on s11, s13, s33 and s44 or C11, C13, C33 and C44. The exact relation is mentioned in the Supplementary information equation S8. Herein, the difference of Young’s modulus upon de-/lithiation along the (100) direction and the perpendicular (001) direction of the LiCoO2 has been calculated. This approximate theoretical prediction is plotted in Fig. 9a in direct comparison to the experimental data.

In general, the DFT prediction of Yamakawa et al. [14] appears shifted to lower absolute values, while the concentration-dependent trends in both orientations align well with the experiment findings as seen in Fig. 9a. In both, DFT calculation and experiment, the Young’s modulus in (003) direction is smaller while exhibiting a sharper decline with delithiation, compared to the (101) direction.

Consequently, the mechanical properties of different LCO orientations upon delithiation, which were previously solely indicated by theoretical calculation [7,14], can now be solidified experimentally. In summary, the theoretical values of the Young moduli appear to underestimate the real ones while the softening with delithiation, especially in the (101) direction, has been slightly overestimated, especially for lower lithium contents. The predicted trend that the (003) orientation shows higher sensitivity to the Li content than the (101) orientation is confirmed by the experiment.

Beside elastic, also the plastic behavior of the material is important, in particular a possible work hardening by cycling. The hardness of the studied films is visualized by Fig. 10 a for the example of the 500 nm layer, for which the substrate effect on hardness is negligible, in direct comparison to the Young’s modulus. In general, the absolute hardness measured here is about three times higher than previously reported in literature [51], where however, hot-pressed LCO pellets were investigated. Thus, the large discrepancy is probably due to the Hall-Petch effect, resulting from the over 100 times smaller grain size in our study in comparison to the cited literature (8.5 µm ± 3.5 µm). Furthermore, the indentation size effect (ISE) could lead to a further increase of measured hardness with decreasing indentation depth [52]. This effect can hardly be avoided in thin film geometry, where the indentation depth has to be kept small to avoid the influence of the substrate. So, to estimate the influence of the limited indentation depth, measurements have been also conducted on a 900 nm thick LCO layer, for which larger indentation depths could be reasonably employed (Fig. 10b). A reduction of hardness by 12.3 % with increasing indentation depth (100 nm), verifies a substantial influence of the ISE. However, in quantitative terms this ISE cannot explain the observed difference by two orders of magnitude between the hardness of the thin film microstructure and hot-pressed pellets. Notably with same indentation depth, the hardness of the 900 nm thick layer is considerably higher than measured for the 500 nm film, which is probably again a consequence of varying lattice orientation.

While the absolute values might still be influenced by less controlled factors, the clear decline in hardness upon delithiation is a solid result that resembles partly the behavior of the elastic modulus. Plastic deformation in hexagonal materials usually appears by glide on the basal plane. So, it comes as a natural explanation that together with the reduced Coulomb interaction between the (003) planes also the glide between these planes become easier.

As the operation of the battery represents a kind of low-cycle fatigue experiment, the plastic behavior during cycling may become a major factor. The change of hardness upon delithiation for differently cycled samples is presented in Fig. 11 . A remarkable increase in hardness with the cycle number can be observed. Furthermore, it is noted, that the sample cycled only once, shows a steeper decrease for low lithium concentrations. Later the dependence on the Li content relaxes, so that the hardness only decreases linearly together with the Li concentration. The phenomenon of increasing hardness with cycle number is probably understood as classical work hardening, since an increase of dislocation density with cycling has been previously reported for cycling LCO to voltage boundaries of 4.35V [53] or 4.7V [54]. This increase in dislocation density is primarily due to repeated phase transformations in LCO [54]. Naturally, also the limited lithium concentration after cycling has an influence on the hardness, although the impact appears to be comparably minor, as it would primarily contribute to a decline in hardness after cycles.

In this context, also the elastic recovery ration (ERR) might be an interesting indicator for the varying yield strength of the material. Eq. (4) has been employed to calculate the ERR and Eq. (3) to estimate the plastic resistance. Corresponding results are shown in Fig. 12 . A clear increase in the ERR and thus an increased plastic strength is demonstrated upon lithiation. It clearly signifies that uptake of Li increases the strength, leading to less plastic and more elastic deformation. This suggests that the plastic hardening upon lithiation is stronger than the impact on elasticity.

For the yield strength, the onset of dislocation glide, has to be considered. Interestingly, although the number of lithium atoms decreases upon delithiation, the overall lattice cell volume remains nearly constant [7]. Consequently, the average free volume between the glide planes has to increase. This presumably results in a decrease in the Peierls force [55], which represents the decisive barrier acting against dislocation slip. Thus, dislocations can move more easily after Li has been removed, fully in line with the observed reduction in the plastic strength.

In this study, the mechanical properties of sputter-deposited thin LCO films were experimentally examined using nanoindentation. The observations can be summarized as follows:
(1)
By combining nanoindentation with texture dependent XRD measurements, the Young’s modulus for pure (003) and (101) oriented samples were determined to be 267.9 ± 7.2 GPa and 337.1 ± 8.7 GPa, respectively.
(2)
The decrease in Young’s modulus measured perpendicular to the (101) or the (003) plane upon delithiation has been compared to theoretical DFT calculations. The calculated decrease with lithium concentration for both directions is in agreement with the experimental data, while the theoretically predicted absolute Young’s modulus values are significantly smaller.
(3)
The sensitivity of Young’s modulus to the grade of lithiation decreases upon long term cycling, due to irreversible phase transitions from the O3-II to the O3-I phase.
(4)
The hardness against plastic deformation was determined to 23.5 GPa for a 500 nm thick fully lithiated sample, much higher than previously reported for pressed pellets of LCO. The difference is likely explained by significant smaller grain size in deposited thin films leading to significant Hall-Petch effect.
(5)
Similar to elastic stiffness, also hardness showed a significant decrease (by 39 %) when the material is delithated down to the possible reversible limit of 50 % state of charge. Remarkably, even the elastic recovery ratio increases with delithiation which indicates that the plastic softening is even stronger than the impact of decreasing Li content on elasticity.
(6)
Opposite to elastic stiffness, hardness increases during prolonged cycling. Obviously repeated phase transformations induce emission of dislocations that lead to substantial work hardening.

The presented results provide valuable information for building an all-solid-state battery, as the texture, lithiation state and cycling of LiCoO2 significantly influences the mechanical properties. This is also relevant in designing an enhanced microstructure as has also been done in a prior report which has been reported to function longer even in a liquid cell [32].

Robert Löser: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yug Joshi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Roham Talei: Data curation. Petra Ebbinghaus: Methodology. Guido Schmitz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) in the frame of a large investment fund for the new transmission microscope (INST 41/1096–1 FUGG, Project number 419370052) and a project fund dedicated to using battery materials as active actuators (SCHM 1182/ 29–1, Photoelektromat, Project number 504493661).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.actamat.2025.121155.
